hero.title: Open-Source Lap Timing & Telemetry for Electric Skateboards
hero.subtitle: Drop in a datalog and explore your laps — maps, charts, sectors and video sync. Everything runs locally in your browser. No upload, no account required.
hero.offlineNote: Works entirely offline now that you’ve visited it!

footer.previewWarning: This branch is running on a preview database — accounts can be wiped at any time. Do not rely on data being saved anywhere other than locally. If payments are activated, do not enter real payment information.
footer.openSource: Open source — GPL-3.0 · View on GitHub

about.title: About RacePlex

offline.heading: Works Offline & Can Be Installed
offline.body: RacePlex is a fully offline-capable web application. Once loaded, it works without an internet connection — perfect for the track. You can install it like a native app on your phone, tablet, or computer by using the "Install" option in your browser menu (or the prompt that appears at the bottom of the page).

data.heading: Your Data Stays on Your Device
data.body: All data processing happens entirely in your browser, and everything is saved locally on your device by default. Cloud storage is entirely optional — nothing leaves your device unless you create an account and turn on sync.

community.heading: Community Track Database
community.body: Don't see your track? You can define custom track and course layouts in the editor, then submit them to the site-wide database for everyone to use. Submissions are reviewed before being added.

oss.heading: Open Source
oss.body: RacePlex is open source under the GPL-3.0, and the source is on GitHub. There is no paid tier, no feature gating and no account: every feature is available to everyone, and the app runs entirely on your own device.

features: Multi-format file support (RaceBox CSV, GPX, VBO, NMEA, UBX, MoTeC, AiM CSV + XRK/XRZ, Alfano, iRacing, Dove, Dovex)
Point-to-point courses (hill runs, slalom, drag) — not just circuits
Lap timing with zero setup: timing lines recovered from the datalog itself
Automatic track & course detection within 5 miles
Automatic driving direction detection (forward/reverse)
Waypoint mode — lap timing anywhere, no track needed
Interactive race line map with speed heatmap
Braking zone detection & visualization
Automatic lap detection via start/finish line
3-sector split timing with optimal lap
Pro graph view with multi-series telemetry charts
G-G diagram (friction circle) for grip-usage analysis
Distance or time chart axis — laps line up by track position
Reference lap overlay & pace delta comparison
Multi-lap overlay — compare laps across the map & graphs at once
Overlay laps from past sessions & other loggers (auto drift-aligned)
Lap snapshots — fastest lap saved per engine & course for comparison
Video sync with telemetry playback
9 overlay gauge types (digital, analog, graph, bar, bubble, map, pace, sector, lap time)
MP4 video export with overlays & audio (H.264 + AAC)
Vehicle profiles & setup sheet management
Frozen setup history — every session keeps the setup it ran
Session notes per file
BLE device integration (DovesDataLogger)
Device track sync over Bluetooth
Over-the-air firmware updates — flash your logger straight from the app
Custom track & course editor with community submissions
Local weather lookup
Dark & light mode
PWA — installable & fully offline
Available in 7 languages
Native Android app — Wi-Fi download from AiM MyChron
Phone-as-logger — GPS lap timing with no extra hardware
Optional cloud sync across your devices (free tier)
AI driving coach (optional add-on)

fileImport.title: Open a datalog
fileImport.dragDrop: Drag & drop a file here, or click to browse
fileImport.formats: GPX, GoPro video (.mp4), NMEA, UBX, VBO, Dove, Alfano, AiM (CSV + XRK/XRZ), iRacing, MoTeC CSV/LD and more — all processed locally on your device.
fileImport.parseErrorSaved: {{message}} — file was saved and can be found in Browse Files.

supportedFiles.title: Supported File Formats
supportedFiles.description: All parsing is done locally in your browser — nothing is uploaded.

dove.name: Dove CSV
dove.body: Simple CSV with millisecond Unix timestamps, GPS data, RPM, and hardware accelerometer readings. The native format of DovesDataLogger. Extension: .dove

dovex.name: Dovex (Extended Dove)
dovex.body: Extended Dove format with a 4096-byte metadata header containing session info (driver, course, lap times) followed by standard Dove CSV GPS data. Extension: .dovex

nmea.name: NMEA / CSV (Tab-Delimited)
nmea.body: Tab-delimited CSV with NMEA sentences — the legacy format used by our earlier custom dataloggers. Extensions: .nmea, .csv, .txt

vbo.name: Racelogic VBO
vbo.body: Racelogic VBOX and RaceBox export format. Extension: .vbo

raceboxCsv.name: RaceBox CSV
raceboxCsv.body: The default export from the RaceBox app (Mini / Mini S / Micro): GPS, speed, G-force and gyro. If the export carries RaceBox's own lap numbering, RacePlex reconstructs the start/finish lines from it, so you get lap times with no setup at all. Note: RaceBox writes the speed column with no unit — RacePlex measures it against your positions to work out whether it's kph, mph or m/s. Extension: .csv

gpx.name: GPX
gpx.body: The universal GPS track format — emitted by nearly every logger, phone app and watch. GPX carries no speed channel, so speed is derived from position and smoothed. If the file contains Start/Finish waypoints (RaceBox writes them), RacePlex rebuilds the timing lines from them and times your run automatically. Extension: .gpx

gopro.name: GoPro Video (GPMF)
gopro.body: Drop the video in. If you filmed your run on a GPS-capable GoPro, the telemetry is already inside the file — HERO5–HERO11 and HERO13 write 10–18 Hz GPS, plus accelerometer and gyro, into the video's GPMF metadata track. RacePlex reads it directly in your browser with gpmf-extract and gopro-telemetry; nothing is uploaded and no conversion step is needed. Note: the HERO12 has no GPS at all, and on older cameras GPS must be switched on in Preferences before recording. Extension: .mp4

vescCsv.name: VESC Tool CSV
vescCsv.body: A VESC ride log — and the reason to use RacePlex on an eskate at all. It brings the ESC channels with it: motor current, battery sag, duty cycle, ERPM and temps land on the same chart as your GPS trace. A nosedive is a duty-cycle event, not a GPS event — a GPS trace only ever shows you the aftermath. The ESC logs about 12x faster than the GNSS, so we keep every ESC row at full rate and interpolate position between fixes, rather than decimating the log to 1 Hz and throwing that resolution away. Extension: .csv

genericCsv.name: Any other CSV
genericCsv.body: Almost any delimited log with a latitude and a longitude in it will import — Float Control, pOnewheel, Metr, TrackAddict, Qstarz, or a logger we have never heard of. RacePlex sniffs the delimiter, maps the columns by name, and then shows you what it decided and lets you fix it before importing. The units it cannot read from a column name — time and speed — are inferred and put in front of you, because a wrong one produces a ride that charts beautifully and is wrong. Your correction is remembered against that device's column layout, so you are only asked once. Extensions: .csv, .txt

ubx.name: u-blox UBX Binary
ubx.body: Binary NAV-PVT messages from u-blox GPS receivers. Extension: .ubx

motecLd.name: MoTeC LD Binary
motecLd.body: Binary data from MoTeC data loggers and sim racing exports (ACC, iRacing, etc.). Extension: .ld

motecCsv.name: MoTeC CSV
motecCsv.body: CSV exports from MoTeC i2 Pro analysis software. Extension: .csv

alfano.name: Alfano CSV
alfano.body: CSV exports from the Alfano ADA app. Extension: .csv

aimCsv.name: AiM MyChron CSV
aimCsv.body: CSV exports from Race Studio 3 (RS2Analysis style) for MyChron 5/6. Extension: .csv

xrk.name: AiM XRK / XRZ (Binary)
xrk.body: AiM's native binary telemetry from MyChron / SoloDL loggers, including the zlib-compressed .xrz variant. Parsed entirely in your browser by libxrk compiled to WebAssembly — fast and fully offline, like every other format. Extensions: .xrk, .xrz

iracing.name: iRacing Telemetry (Binary)
iracing.body: iRacing's native binary telemetry export — the same data the sim's live irsdk API serves, no third-party CSV/MoTeC conversion needed. Read directly in your browser (fully offline): GPS position, speed and altitude drive the map and laps, with throttle, brake, gear, steering, RPM, water/oil temp and native lateral/longitudinal g along for the ride. Extension: .ibt

supportedDevices.title: Supported Devices
supportedDevices.description: Anything that exports GPX, CSV, VBO, NMEA or UBX works — and so does a GoPro .mp4, because the GPS is already inside the video. An unrecognised CSV still imports: RacePlex maps the columns and lets you correct it. RacePlex sells no hardware and is affiliated with none of these vendors — a device is listed because it works.
supportedDevices.footer: Missing a device? The list lives in src/data/supported-devices.json — open a pull request. If you own hardware we haven’t tested, sending us a sample export file is the single most useful contribution you can make.
